text: The video shows the robotic conversion from a gastric band to a sleeve gastrectomy. Initially, laparoscopic mobilization frees the port attachment to the tubing. The robot is introduced, using a Cotillier retractor on the left and scissors on the right, enhancing mobilization and reticulation. The X-ray robotic arm aids in traction and counter-traction during dissection. Scar tissue is carefully dissected from liver adhesions to prevent injury to the stomach's cirrhosis. The left lobe of the liver is fully mobilized, and scar tissue attachments are meticulously removed.

The surgeon identifies the line of transection and begins taking down the gastro-gastric stitches using three robotic arms. The simultaneous adjustment of traction and counter traction aids in mobilizing and freeing up the plane for the gastro-gastric connection.

The surgeon mobilizes the greater omentum along the greater curvature, measuring approximately five centimeters proximal to the pylorus. The vessel sealer is used to dissect and open the greater omentum to access the lesser sac, revealing virgin tissue planes with no adhesions.

The surgeon opens and removes the band, mobilizes the capsule where the band was located, and uses scissors with traction and counter traction to safely open the capsule. A Forty French Visigy Bougie is advanced towards the pylorus, and a black stapler is used to begin the sleeve formation.

The surgeon over-sews the staple line in a Lambert manner, incorporating a portion of the gray omentum to buttress the line. A methylene blue test is performed, showing no leakage, and the removed specimen is displayed.
